stem_zh: 3. Para Fuels Co's cost of capital has been used as the discount rate for both projects.This assumption may not be accurate.A risk-adjusted,project specific cost of capital would give a more accurate estimate of the value of the project

4. When calculating the value of the offer made by Kero Innovations Co,using the BSOP model,the standard deviation or volatility of the underlying asset is needed,and the BSOP model assumes that this can be estimated accurately. This may be possible for frequently traded products,but this probably is not the case for one-off projects and new projects.Such estimation is difficult to do and is open to significant errors.Therefore,the assumption of the accuracy of the volatility measure is questionable. 5.The BSOP model assumes that markets are frictionless and the project's cash flows follow a lognormal distribution, both of which are reasonable assumptions for market-traded products but not necessarily for investment projects. 6.The BSOP model assumes that,once determined,the variables will not change,but in reality,they may do.

7. In order to estimate the value of Kero Innovations Co's offer for Investment B,Para Fuels Co has assumed that the offer is binding,but currently it is only a potential offer. The estimates of value resulting from the calculations in the appendices should therefore be treated as indicative and not definitive. (iv)Response to the BoD meeting on the replacement of the production facility Adherence to environmental,social and governance(ESG)aspects are becoming increasingly important criteria by which to judge a company as a good corporate citizen,from the perspective of all stakeholder groups,including investors. Whether Para Fuels Co decides to undertake the replacement of the production facility with traditional technology which uses crude oil or the new technology using household waste,this will impact on the company's expected environmental responsibilities,the perception of how it discharges these and thereby impacting its reputation as a good corporate citizen. This is probably what the chief executive officer is referring to.However,the BoD should equally be cautious about pursuing an agenda which compromises Para Fuels Co's financial strength. As the chief marketing officer suggests,airline companies will be concerned about their responsibilities with respect to the environment and using this type of jet fuel would be of significant help in achieving a high level of environmental protection.Therefore,both Para Fuels Co and its customers,the airline companies,are in agreement.The airlines will be naturally concerned about the safety aspects of the new product.However,once the new type of jet fuel is proven to be as safe as the current type of jet fuel,these concerns should dissipate and sales revenue will probably increase rapidly. The chief financial officer (CFO)has put forward the argument that financially Para Fuels Co does not benefit from moving to the new technology currently.Furthermore,whereas there should be a positive uplift once the fuel is deemed safe,the current computations do not necessarily show this.The CFO appears to be concerned that if more production facilities are replaced with the new technology,this will reduce the company's and its shareholders'value.Nonetheless,it is possible that over the coming two years,Para Fuels Co will have a much better knowledge of the new type of jet fuel and its safety. Therefore,the computations will be more reliable and might well show a substantial positive value creation impact. The significant advantage for Para Fuels Co,if it went with the new technology,is that as an early adopter it should benefit from positive learning curve effects and ways to make the production of the jet fuel more efficient,than would be the case if they continue with the traditional technology.This would result in the reduction of ongoing costs.The set-up costs in the future may also reduce,if the company establishes better ways of constructing production facilities needed in two years' time.Revenues may also increase as Para Fuels Co will have more time to build better customer networks and relations, and a better marketing strategy,all built around the fuel coming from the adoption of the new technology. How should Para Fuels Co proceed If the new technology is adopted for the single production facility initially,Para Fuels Co may wish to proceed as follows: 1.The company should launch a public relations(PR)campaign to highlight its environmental credentials,build its reputation and thereby hopefully increase its corporate value; 2.The company should also launch a PR campaign to reassure its investors that it aims to maximise long-term corporate value and this move to new technology is very much in keeping with that; 3.Since there may be safety concerns initally,the company may want to support research investigations to assess the safety of the fuel,including financial support,especially in conjunction with airline companies involved in this research and possibly other interested companies,such as Kero Innovations Co;

4. The company should put into place effective processes to maximise learning opportunities from the investment made in the first production facility.It should then use this learning to reduce costs and to produce more accurate estimations and,where possible,reduce uncertanties of cash flow forecasts; 5.The company should ask Kero Innovations Co to make a formal,contractual offer and possibly align it to two years, when it will need to make further decisions,in case the new technology proves not to be feasible. If Para Fuels Co decides to stick with the traditional technology instead,this technology is sill environmentally friendly and the PR campaigns externally and to investors should proceed.As a result,Para Fuels Co will hopefully maintain its corporate reputation and value. Conclusion The report suggests that based only on financial grounds,and taking into account assumptions made,Para Fuels Co should replace the production facility with traditional technology.However,the new technology does offer significant opportunities, especilly in terms of environmental benefits,and therefore suggestions are put forward of how Para Fuels Co might proceed. Gahana Co pays USD14·74m to Adalar Co. Main advantage of multilateral netting Multilateral netting is not technically a method of managing exposure to foreign exchange risk.It is a cost saving mechanism, which enables Lough Co to minimise the number of settlement transactions by netting off intra-group balances before settlement is made.This will significantly reduce the transaction costs associated with intra-group setlements in a way that is congruent with the chief executive office's(CEO's)new cost reduction strategy. (b)Supplier payment in Swedish krona The forward market indicates the Swedish krona is expected to depreciate and it may be more beneficial not to hedge atall, depending on the board's attitude to risk. Forward market hedge SEK125m/8·5308=USD14,652,788 Money market hedge Deposit:SEK125m/(1+[0·021×5/12])=SEK123,915,737 Convert:SEK123,915,737/8·4458=USD14,671,877 Borrow:USD14,671,877x(1+[0·022x5/12])=USD14,806,368 Both hedging methods result in a fixed payment.The forward market hedge minimises the payment whilst also being administratively less complex and would therefore be the preferred choice. (c) Cost reduction strategy The centralised treasury function contributes to Lough Co's cost reduction strategy by exploiting significant economies of scale, therefore reducing average costs and increasing shareholder value.Scale economies avoid the unnecessary duplication of skills across the group and concentrate the expertise required within a central department.If Lough Co decentralises the treasury function it would be much more expensive to replicate the same degree of expertise in each subsidiary.A centralised treasury will also have a much greater awareness of the company's overallrisk exposure and will have the resources and expertise required to manage those risks more effectively.Scale economies reduce Lough Co's cost of debt since better interest rates can be negotiated when borrowing in bulk and since funds are remitted back to head office and managed centrally,surplus funds will also attract better rates of interest compared to those available for smaller deposits managed locally. Another benefit is that funds can also be transferred internally from subsidiaries with spare cash resources to the subsidiaries which need them,which avoids the unnecessary expense of raising finance on the external market.In addition,as seen in thee scenario,a centralised treasury is able to undertake mulilateral netting,which reduces the costs associated with hedging. Finance director's proposal The disadvantages of a centralised treasury department are highlighted in the finance director's briefing note.As evidenced by the experience of Lough Co's Indian subsidiary,decision making may take too long to be effective when the treasury is centralised.Local managers would be able to respond much more quickly to local developments and may have avoided thee delay in funding and hence the missed opportunity experienced by Gahana Co.More autonomous decision making would also increase staff motivation and may help to avoid the current problems with staff turnover which have been experienced across the group.Increased motivation improves Lough Co's profit making potential,thereby increasing shareholder value. Decentralisation delegates decision making authority to those individuals who have a greater feel for local conditions.The low take-up of the initial public offering(IPO)in Turkey is another example of a missed opportunity.The lack of attention paid to the needs of local investors seems to have been a major contributory factor to that failure.A decentralised treasury function would have been able to build relationships with potential nvestors and would have had a greater understanding of their needs without incurring the unnecessary expense associated with a failed PO. The finance director's suggestion to decentralise the treasury would have avoided many of the problems which have been experienced by Lough Co's subsidiaries but seems incongruent with the CEO's new cost reduction strategy.However,the problems experienced by Lough Co's centralised treasury are also incurring significant costs.In the absence of further information,the board will need to weigh up the costs and benefits of both treasury structures before making a final decision. 3 (a) Scenario 1 Sale of individual assets Realisable value =60%x75%x($7,570,000-$970,000)=$2,970,000 Scenario 2 Close woodcraft shops and keep craft centres open Year 工 2 3 4 5 $000 $000 $000 $000 $000 After-tax profits/Free cash flows 470 494 516 536 558 Discount factor(12%) 0 ·893 0 ·797 0 ·712 0 ·636 0 ·567 Discounted free cash flows 420 394 367 341 316 Present value of free cash flows Years 1 to 5=$1,838,000 Present value of free cash flows Year 6 onwards =($558,000/0·12)x0·567=$2,637,000 Total valuation =$800,000+$1,838,000+$2,637,000=$5,275,000 No adjustment is made for additional investment as it equals depreciation. Scenario 3 Carry on current business and invest Year 1 $000 2 $000 3 $000 After-tax profits 380 420 440 Add:Depreciation 170 190 200 Less:Additional investment (300) (250) (210) Free cash flows 250 360 430 Discount factor(12%) 0 ·893 0 ·797 0 ·712 Discounted free cash flows 223 287 306 Present value of free cash flows Years 1 to 3=$816,000 Present value of free cash flows Year 4 onwards=($430,000x1 ·03)/(0·12-0·03)x0·712=$3,504,000 Total valuation =$816,000+$3,504,000-$600,000=$3,720,000 (b)Sale of individual assets The value achieved by selling off the assets on an individual basis should be regarded as a floor value for the woodcraft business.The figures for the realisable value of the assets may be uncertain,possibly being more or less than forecast. This option does have the advantage of bringing in a quick cash receipt,which can help to fund the investment expenditure which Felinhen Co has to make.If Felinhen Co is adopting a strategy of focusing on core activities,the sale makes its situation simpler and frees up management time which would have been required for the woodcraft business.However,if the woodcraft business could become profitable again,selling it of in this way represents the loss of a potential future income stream.The realisable value of the assets is also lower than the value of the other strategies being considered,meaning it is questionable whether it is the best option for Felinhen Co. This option is also likely to have the biggest reputation risk,as shown already by the adverse media coverage. Close woodcraft shops and keep craft centres open This policy offers a smaller quick cash receipt than the sale of all the individual assets,although again the amount would be dependent on the realisable value of the assets sold. Long term,however,this option appears to offer the highest value for Felinhen Co.The future profit figures will,however,be subject to some uncertainties.It is questionable how well the products would sell in other locations.There is no information about how successful online selling has been for the countryside store products and the products being sold are much bulkier, suggesting the need for investment in vehicles and higher distribution costs.The valuation is dependent on the assumption that the business carries on in perpetuity,which may be unrealistic.There is also no justification given for the cost of capital. This option would keep the craft centres open,mitigating the reputation risk.The board would have to consider the more complicated logistics of trading the wood products in the ways proposed.Aditional storage facilities may be required,given that the woodcraft shops would no longer be available to store items.There is also the logistical problem of countryside stores having to accommodate much larger items such as furniture,limiting the products which can be displayed. Sale to buy-out group The sale at the price offered again is a quick cash receipt. The price offered of $3m is not far above the forecast income from selling the assets of the business individually,which should be regarded as the lowest acceptable price for the woodcraft business.It is possible that the individual assets could sell for more than expected and the total receipt might be higher than the current offer from the buy-out team.However,it appears to be certain that Felinhen Co would receive the $3m if it accepts the buy-out group's offer,whereas it may not achieve the $3m if the assets are sold individually. The calculation for carrying on the business and investing gives an indication of the potential of the woodcraft business if the required investment is made in it,and it is 24%higher than the offer made by the buy-out group.However,the same comments as above apply to carrying on the value to perpetuity and using a cost of capital which has not been justified.Thee valuation suggested by the calculation may well not be acceptable to the purchasers.Theirplans for the business may be based on different and less optimistic assumptions,indicating their lower valuation of $3m is realistic.If the forecast cash flows are realistic,that would suggest that the woodcraft business sill could have significant longer-term value for Felinhen Co,even if significant immediate expenditure is needed. Selling the woodcraft business as a whole would also not involve Felinhen Co having to take decisions about shutting shops and craft centres,avoiding the risk to its reputation. Other options The decision is based on the woodcraft business making a loss in a single year,after a number of years of profitability.It appears that the decision to close or sell the business may be premature.Given that it is forecast to make profits with further investment,there may be a possibility that itcould make profits even without this investment in the coming year,although there has been a decline in profits prior to making the loss.Felinhen Co's board should certainly consider the possibility of leting the woodcraft business operate for at least another year.It is questionable whether that would be a riskier strategy than any of the others proposed. The board should also consider partial implementation of some of the options stated.The valuation calculation indicates that keeping the craft centres and shops open and investing in them has a significantly lower value than just keeping thee craft centres open.However,the board could consider just shutting the worst-performing shops.If the most profitable shops were kept open and investment was made in them,then,as well as making a positive contribution,they could also act as a showcase for the products and a collection hub for online sales. The woodcraft shops'profitability might be improved,and a potential extra sales outlet opened,if some of the products currently sold in the countryside stores were also sold in the woodcraft shops. Felinhen Co's board has generally not considered the links and any synergies between the two businesses.Arguably also,if it wanted to focus on its agricultural and woodland management,it should analyse the possility of divesting of all of Counwood Co and not just its woodcraft business. Recommendation The sale of the business to the buy-out group would provide immediate funds for other required purposes and appears to be a marginally better option than selling the assets individually.It would also mean that Felinhen Co did not suffer the negative publicity through closing the craft centres and shops.Although just keeping the craft centres open and selling their products elsewhere may be a better long-term financial option,the financial and operational uncertainties are likely to be too great for the board to tolerate and also would not result in the desired focus of activities. Felinhen Co's board should negotiate with the buy-out group to try to obtain a higher price than offered,though they should consider setling for a lower price than suggested by the valuation of $3,720,000,which may be over-optimistic. Recommendations are practical and plausible in the context of Felinhen Co's situation - Effective use of examples and/or calculations from the scenario information and other practical considerations related to the context to illustrate points being made Maximum 5marks Answers Strategic Professional-Options,AFM Advanced Financial Management(AFM) September/December 2024 Sample Answers 1 Report to directors of Northney Co on the group treasury function and establishment of new subsidiary Introduction This report discusses the issues with the proposed relocation of the group treasury function to Europe.I illustrates the role of the treasury function by looking at examples of the work which it does.It also discusses the political and operational risks which may impact upon the establishment of a new subsidiary. (i)Relocation of group treasury function to Europe Northney Co will have to decide whether to expand the current treasury function of one of the subsidiaries.Expanding one of the existing functions rather than setting up a new base may be less time-consuming and less costly,as fewer staff may have to be recruited.On the other hand,it may be more convenient to establish the function in a major financial centre in Europe. The working arrangements of the function may also influence where it is established.Currently treasury staf are office-based, but the move to a new centre may prompt more remote working by staf.Other factors may influence where the function is located,including the language skills employees require,the time zone and the legal jurisdiction. Northney Co must consider how the activities of the group treasury function in Europe should be controlled by its head office in the US.Some mechanisms,for example,reports of activities to the board willremain the same.However,the senior management structure will need to be considered-assuming the group finance director stays in America,will a senior person with responsibility for strategic decisions relating to the function be required in Europe?Northney Co should also determine how assurance about treasury activities is obtained.It may not be convenient for the group's internal audit function to carry out the work in Europe,and specialist external assurance may be required. Other treasury functions Northney Co should also review the responsibilities of the treasury functions of subsidiaries in Europe.Previously,their level of autonomy may have been influenced by convenience for subsidiaries,with the group treasury function being established on a different continent.Now they are on the same continent,it may be better that work previously done by the subsidiaries' treasury functions is done by the group function,to reduce duplication of operations and allow for concentration of expertise. Northney Co should,however,consider the wider issue of whether subsidiaries'autonomy isaffected by removing some of thee responsibilities of their treasury functions. Northney Co must also address what happens about treasury activities relating to the American parent company.It will have its own treasury function,which may have more autonomy than local subsidiary functions currently have because of its position at the head of the group.However,the board must be careful to ensure that there is a clear split of responsibilities between thee group treasury function and the parent's treasury function. (v)Methods of hedging The level of receipts which both methods gave in comparison with other methods would have been a factor,though no details are given of the results of forward methods for each transaction.Both methods use traded derivatives,which can be bought and sold as required.They have the security of being traded on regulated markets,which have clearing houses which ensure that obligations are honoured and there is no counterparty risk.Northney Co's group treasury function would have the experience and knowledge to use traded derivatives successfully.The fact that the futures can hedge the JPY receipt exactly may have been a factor in choosing them. Both methods have been chosen in preference to options.This may have been because of the need to pay a large premium on options,and the probability of a favourable rate movement meaning that not exercising the option would be the best choice being considered too low.However,a collar may have been chosen in preference to interest rate futures because it allows somee potential to take advantage of favourable interest rate movements,while limiting the premium paid. Both methods have been chosen making assumptions about basis and margin payments.In practice,there will be initial margin requirements and the possibility of additional margin payments,as well as basis not changing as assumed.Thee assumption must be that these factors are not sufficiently serious to change the decisions about which instrument to use. (vi)Political risks Given that the country has already imposed some restrictions on employment practices,it is possible that more restrictions could be imposed on Northney Co if it establishes a subsidiary there.These could include requirements for a minimum %of shares to be held by local shareholders,possibly complicating decision-making.There could also be economic restrictions,for example,limitations on the transfer of funds out of the country,imposed as part of a change in economic policy. If Northney Co wishes to establish itself as a manufacturer in the local region,it could face the risk of stricter measures being adopted if it exports from its current choice of country to countries in the free trade area.The free trade area could impose bureaucracy,legal requirements or tariffs on goods coming from outside the free trade area.It could also impose tariffs on exports by Northney Co.The impact of these measures may be greater if relations between the suggested country and free trade area worsen.Possibly Northney Co should consider locating its subsidiary in one of the countries within the free trade area. Although it may be subject to stricter regulations,the trading environment may be more stable. Northney Co may also face dificulties if the suggested country has to change its economic policies and bring down its level of debt.Local interest rates may rise,depressing demand and resulting in a higher finance cost if the subsidiary seeks to borrow. Taxes,including business taxes,may need to rise.If the country is viewed as following questionable economic policies,its exchange rate may become unstable,increasing Northney Co's exposure to foreign exchange risk. Operational risks Northney Co may find it difficult to implement its advanced working practices in the suggested country.It may be difficult to recruit suficient local staff with the knowledge of advanced manufacturing and information technology required.The low regulation culture in the country may be reflected in attitudes to working practices,for example,pooratitudes to health and safety.As wellas the risk of injury,health and safety incidents could also lead to disruption in production and a threat to Northney Co's reputation as an employer. The risk of cultural differences affecting operations may be limited by the requirement to employ local staff,who willhave knowledge of the customer base and local business practices.There is,however,the risk that head office dealings with local management and staff may be difficult,particularly if there is a lack of communication of corporate culture and values and if expatriate staff working for the subsidiary lack sensitivity to local cultural issues. Although the extreme weather may help the subsidiary's sales,it may also pose threats to its operations.Supply chains could be disrupted and there could be threats to the premises in which the subsidiary is based. Conclusion This report has discussed the issues associated with relocation of the group treasury function to Europe,emphasising that the roles of other treasury functions in the group and changes in control mechanisms need careful consideration.It has also looked at examples of the netting and hedging activities undertaken by the treasury function.Lastly,it has considered the significant political and operational risks which may impact upon the new subsidiary if it is established. However,the increase in shareholder wealth is much lower for Mortexa Co's shareholders,mainly because the acquisition premium is significantly more generous than it needs to be based on current expectations.This suggests there is room to revise and improve the terms of the offer.On the other hand,Yekkon Co's shareholders may raise their expectations about the acquisition premium if the rumours that a rival is planning its own bid prove credible and this could potentially threaten the viability of the acquisition.On the basis of current expectations,Yekkon Co shareholders are likely to be much more enthusiastic about the acquisition compared to Mortexa Co shareholders but this could change depending on thee credibility of those rumours. The results depend to a large extent on the chief executive officer's (CEO)assumptions about the pre-and post-acquisition price earnings ratios.The assumption behind Yekkon Co's pre-acquisition PE ratio is questionable,mainly due to the fact that the company is unlisted whereas Mortexa Co's shares are traded on a stock exchange.All other variables being equal, it would be expected for the valuation of an unlisted company to be at a discount to that of a listed company due to thee lack of marketability. (b) According to the scenario,eficiency gains are predicted on the basis that the combined company will be able to negotiate better terms and lower costs from suppliers whilst also achieving cost savings where functional areas are duplicated,for example, head office overheads.In theory,there is an argument that these gains could benefit the consumer by being passed on in the form of lower prices.However,this assumes the sector is sill competitive enough for supermarket chains to have that incentive. Another significant benefit may arise from the increased investment in new mobile technology,which will llow Mortexa Co to compete more effectively with online supermarkets whilst also improving service quality to consumers.This investment should also provide a source of earnings growth and,more significantly for the Competition Authority,could potentially create new jobs.The information provided by Mortexa Co's CEO about the size and significance of this investment is quite vague but the Competition Authority may want to investigate the potential for job creation further.However,it is not obvious why this investment can only take place if the acquisition is approved. In spite of the above,the market share test indicates there may be grounds for competition concerns.If Mortexa Co achieves significant market power because of its size,it may be able to shield itselfrom competition and increase prices to the detriment of consumers.The main supermarket chains may not always be represented in alltowns and smaller cities,which means in some areas the market share will be even higher than the 26%predicted in the scenario.If Mortexa Co consolidates branches, particularly in the less populated areas,consumer choice could be restricted even further. On the other hand,the combined market share only exceeds the 25%threshold very slightly and so the market share test may not be regarded as an insurmountable impediment.The market share test may also need to be applied flexibly to allow for marginal cases,otherwise a merger with 25·1%market share would be automatically blocked whereas a merger with 24·9% would not even though there is very little between the two.Mortexa Co's market share relative to its competitors will also be an important factor,particularly as there has been a surge in merger and acquisition activity within the industry over the last decade. Lannes'Competition Authority may have concerns about the supplier cost savings mentioned previously since these may be exploitative,achieved through the misuse of market power rather than genuine efficiency gains.If Mortexa Co plans to eliminate duplicate costs,the Competition Authority may be concerned about the number of job losses. The FCFs method of estimating the equity value of Zulla Co is better than the dividend method.The dividend method assumes that only dividends drive the value of a company.However,Zulla Co appears to be a growing company with the FCFs being significantly higher than dividends and growing at a faster rate.Therefore FCFs are a more accurate driver of value for Zulla Co, which as a growing company seems to be retaining cash for future investment. Nonetheless,both methods used in the calculations of equity value are simplistic.For example,it is not clear in case of dividends how the growth rate is determined,or in the case of FCFs,why the future growth rate should be based on the past growth rate.It is also unclear whether or not the cost of capital and the cost of equity are accurate.A small change in these variables will result in a large change in Zulla Co's equity value. A much deeper analysis is warranted and accurate future cash flow figures need to be determined in order to accurately assess Zulla Co's equity value. (c) The initial public offering(IPO)is the conventional way to obtain a listing where ZullaCo would issue and offer its shares to the public through a stock exchange.With a reverse takeover (RTO),Zulla Co's shares would be exchanged for the non-trading listed company's shares,but because Zulla Co has control of the non-trading listed company,it would effectively get a listing on the stock exchange where the non-trading company is listed. The RTO process can be cheaper and quicker for Zulla Co compared to the conventional IPO.The RTO is cheaper because it avoids the costs of hiring financial experts who are typically involved in the IPO process,it avoids the costs of marketing the sale of its shares and it avoids the costs related to compliance process of undertaking an IPO.The RTO is quicker because it does not need the time the IPO process requires when undertaking the compliance process and selling shares to the public.These two factors are presumably the burden which Zulla Co's chief operating officer is hoping to avoid,although it is noteworthy that RTOs have become less popular in recent years,although there has been significant growth in listing via special-purpose acquisition companies(SPACs). Furthermore,an IPO may not be successful if there is an insufficient interest by investors or the price per share is deemed too high.Given that Zulla Co has an excellent reputation,as long as it prices its shares at a reasonable level,this should not be an issue and there should be sufficient investor interest. On the other hand,Zulla Co needs to consider the burdens an RTO would face which are not present if undertaking an IPO. When taking control of the non-trading listed company,Zulla Co needs to undertake suficient due diligence of that company to ensure that it is not facing any hidden problems like potential future litigation against it. Additionally,Zulla Co may find that it isdificult to raise new funds easily.It is likely that the non-trading listed company has no investor fllowing and therefore obtaining a listing through an RTO does not guarantee Zulla Co instant investor following and access to new funds quickly,especially as a reverse merger may lack credibility with investors as it can be seen as a short-cut to achieve a listing.Given that access to funds is the main reason cited by Zulla Co for undertaking a listing,this may prove to be problematic.On the other hand,its excellent reputation may ensure that this is not an issue and new investors are drawn to it readily. In summary,if Zulla Co undertakes effective due diligence and if it dovetails its high reputation to gain a sufficient investor following,then the RTO may be the less burdensome and more beneficial process. Management(AFM) March/June 2024 Sample Answers 1 (a)Rationale for protectionist measures Many countries impose protectionist measures on imported goods and services to make them more expensive to buy,therefore restricting demand.In doing so,they aim to shield or protect their own industries and workers from foreign competition. It may be that these are infant industries,which are still developing and,given time,have the potential to compete globally whereas exposure to the full force of international competition in the early years of the industry life cycle could damage that development ireparably.Amasia's car manufacturing industry may be an example of such an industry.Alternatively,thee objective may be to protect old industries,such as Amasia's agricultural sector.International competition may accelerate the process by which these industries decline and ultimately collapse,creating the potential for large-scale unemployment in Amasia and all of the problems that can cause. The tax revenue derived from imposing import tarifs,for example,may be a major consideration for Amasia,given the fact that there is a substantial budget deficit.For emerging markets in particular,this revenue can be significant.By restricting thee demand for imports,protectionist measures may also avoid the problems associated with a worsening trade deficit,which occurs when the value of a country's imports exceeds the value of its exports. Benefits from customs union A customs union creates a trading bloc which removes protectionist measures on imports from one country to other countries within the bloc.The key benefit is an increase in trade and economic growth across the bloc as a whole and this in turn should help with easing Amasia's unemployment problem.The customs union also enables countries to specialise in those industries in which they have a comparative advantage,which can create the potential for a global competitive advantage. Companies operating within a customs union gain access to a substantially larger market.This creates a cost advantage due to the economies of scale associated with larger markets and this ultimately benefits consumers in the form of lower prices. It is not unusual for a country entering into a customs union to negotiate exceptions.Therefore it may be possible for Amasia to enjoy the benefits of a custom union without removing the protections from its infant and declining industries. (b)Report to the board of directors,Mahoney Co Introduction This report assesses how Mahoney Co can manage the risk associated with various transactions.In particular,it evaluates the policy to use multilateral netting to settle intra-group balances and compares the methods used to hedge both Oliviera Co's disposal proceeds and the Amasian project's cash flows.The report then discusses the assumptions made before concluding on the suitablity of the project in Amasia. Intra-group settlements The cash flow impact from Mahoney Co's multilateral netting policy is as follows: According to Appendix 1,Yves Co pays €14·6m to Mahoney Co and then €132·4m to Oliviera Co.Imran Co pays €82·4m to Oliviera Co. Benefits of multilateral nettng Multilateral netting allows Mahoney Co to substantially reduce the number of cross-border transfers within the group.This saves time and also reduces the transaction costs associated with intra-group settlements.Any risk of non-payment is also spread across the group. Potential problems associated with multilateral netting There are a number of disadvantages associated with the plan to introduce multilateral netting.First,there may be legal issues to consider because some countries impose restrictions on mulilateral netting to protect their financial sector.Second, settlements can be made at any forward date agreed to by the group but six months seems excessive.Finally,if Oliviera Co is sold,as planned,in six months'time,the terms of sale would need to be investigated to check whether the settlement is still due. Hedging of Oliviera Co's disposal proceeds Both the forward and future markets suggest there may be a depreciation of the BRL relative to the euro over the coming months.This would result in a material decline in the euro value of the disposal proceeds if the transaction was not hedged. Given the magnitude of the amount due,even very small movements in the exchange rate could lead to a material loss for Mahoney Co under these circumstances,which suggests it is advisable to hedge since the proceeds are due to be used to repay the bridging loan. Appendix 2 shows both hedging methods result in broadly similar outcomes although the futures market outcome is marginally better compared to the forward.Since the amount to be hedged is not known with certainty and there is also a risk the transaction may not happen atall,the futures hedge may be more attractive as the contracts can be sold before the maturity date,whereas the forward must be fufilled.In theory,both methods fix the exchange rate but in practice futures contracts are exposed to basis risk since Mahoney Co will lose out the September contracts two months before maturity.Basis risk creates uncertainty about the actual futures market outcome,which may end up being more or less than the estimated receipt of €122·38m.Futures contracts also suffer from a cash flow disadvantage,since they are marked-to-market daily until the futures contracts are closed out. If the board is risk averse,the forward may be the preferred hedging method since the amount received is only marginally lower and would remove any uncertainty for the purposes of repaying the bridging loan.However,the directors will ned to assess the exposure to counterparty risk first. Regeneration project in Amasia Currency swap and currency option outcomes Given the depreciation of the dollar over the investment period,the currency swap rate is clearly beneficial.A significant portion of the project's value is wrapped up in the final year cash flow,mainly due to the transfer proceeds received at the end of the investment period.Without the currency swap,these proceeds would be converted into euros at the final year expected exchange rate of $/17·94,which is substantially less favourable than the rate agreed to under the terms of the swap.That said,Appendix 3 shows that the project does not appear to be viable even with the benefit of a swap since the net present value(NPV)is negative.The currency swap is also exposed to counterparty risk. The outcome from the currency option is significantly worse,mainly due to the cost of the premium.Based on the information provided,the option would be exercised at the end of the project.Unlike the currency swap,however,the option benefits from upside potential.If the dollar strengthens sufficiently,it is possible the NPV would be positive.However,this seems unlikely based on purchasing power parity theory,which predicts a significant weakening of the dollar. Assumptions It is assumed that the inputs provided are credible.For example,it is assumed that the investment cost,net operating fee, transfer proceeds,spot rate,inflation rates,swap rate,discount rate and the option premium are estimated accurately.It is also assumed that the discount rate remains constant throughout.Minor changes to any of these inputs may be sufficient to change the outcome of the calculations. It is worth investigating these assumptions,particularly when the NPV is so marginal.For example,the operating fee is relatively static throughout the investment period but if Amasia joins a customs union with its neighbours,there is the potential for a significant increase in trade.The operating fee is based on the volume of traffic through the port so this input may increase more than the estimates provided suggest. There may also be room to improve the terms of the swap or to find an alternative swap partner on more favourable terms. Given that the NPV is only very slightly negative,a very small improvement in these terms may be enough to turn the NPV positive.For example,there may be room to negotiate the rate at which the swap of principal occurs at the end of the project. Alternatively,it may be worth negotiating the transfer proceeds with the Amasian government. The NPV may also understate the true value of the project since this is only the first phase of the por's regeneration.Thee experience Mahoney Co gains on this project may lead to additional work during subsequent phases of the port's regeneration. This is an example of a real option which has the potential to add value to a project but is ignored by conventional NPV methodology. Conclusion The intra-group settlement from Yves Co and the disposal proceeds from the divestment of Oliviera Co are expected to generate a receipt in excess of €136m in six months'time.The board will need to compare this amount to the value of the bridging loan to identify any shortfall. Based on the financial analysis,the regeneration project in Amasia should be rejected since the NPV is negative.However, the value of the project may be understated since it seems to ignore a potential follow-on option.Furthermore,the NPV is only marginally negative so it may be possible to negotiate the terms of the swap with the counterparty or the value of the transfer proceeds expected from the Amasian government in a way which would lead to a positive NPV.Since the value of the project is only a marginal loss,it is also recommended that the board undertake sensitivity and scenario analysis. (b) Diversification has two aspects:product and market development. Product development Supplying schools and universities would represent a limited product diversification by producing some different products to what would be supplied for offices.However,Littlebredy Co would remain a supplier of furniture,as it is for offices.For both schools and offices,supply would be a combination of replacing existing furniture and equipment for current buildings,which is likely to be a steady market,and supplying new furniture and equipment,either for new buildings or because of technological developments or other factors affecting what customers require. Market development Supplying schools and universities is a different market from supplying offices,but how much this represents effective diversification will be determined by how much demand in these markets is influenced by the same factors.To some extent, economic circumstances will determine demand in both markets.Poor economic circumstances will limit new office buildings and demand from existing offices,and may also limit government tax revenues,capping the amount which can be spent on education. However,political imperatives,here the priority given to education,willhave a significant impact on the demand from educational institutions,with demand from offices much less likely to be affected by government policies.Other government policies,such as whether contracts are awarded on a competitive,open-tender basis or given to preferred,government-linked suppliers,may determine how far Littebredy Co can access the education sector The upgrade of furniture and equipment for technological developments is likely to be at a different pace for offices and educational establishments so it is possible that this represents effective diversification for Littlebredy Co. (c)Provision of more information As a first stage,Litlebredy Co needs to communicate better with major shareholders to find out what their requirements are. These may result in Litlebredy Co producing more information than legislation and the governance code require,for example, more detail about the impact of government decisions about education on the demand for its products,and more performance indicators than are currently shown in its annual report and business review. It is true that commercial confidentiality may restrict the reporting of plans for specific investments while they are being formulated,but Lilebredy Co should be able to outline the major areas of product and market development which it is pursuing to fulfi its strategies. Business review In particular,it appears that Litlebredy Co's board is taking a minimal approach to disclosure of information about its planned strategies.Although it is doing enough to fulfil legislative requirements and has produced a business review in line with thee corporate governance code,the principles upon which the code is based would require the business review to be meaningful. This would mean that the business review should discuss the investment decisions which Litlebredy Co has made in thee context of its overall business objectives and strategies going forward. Litlebredy Co may decide to produce an integrated report,meaning that it would need to discuss how its investment strategies will deliver value over various timeframes in the future.It will also need to describe the business model underpinning its investment policies. (Note:Credit will be given for alternative and valid discursive comments) 3 (a)Strategic fit The most important reason for acquiring either company is strategic fit,whether they would enhance what Garnod Co currently offers or offer opportunities for further development.Although Pilsur Co appears to operate in a complementary area to Garnod Co,in some ways it does not appear attractive.It may be easier for Garnod Co to develop law and tax compliance courses itself with its existing resources and recruiting extra staff required individually,rather than going through the formalities and likely conflicts in acquiring Pilsur Co,a company which has not been very successful recently.The main area in which Garnod Co could develop Pilsur Co's business is e-learning.Here,Garnod Co's success in developing e-learning in its area could complement Pilsur Co. Marhew Co offers wider opportunities to provide professional development courses to Garnod Co's existing clients.It also offers a new client base for Garnod Co to sell its own courses.Much of Marhew Co's business is personal skills training,where it may be more difficult to develop e-learning packages.However,there may be scope to develop packages in areas where lots of people have to be trained,for example,health and safety courses.Marhew Co has also been more successful at developing its business recently and it is quite possible that its team is more motivated than Pilsur Co's staff. Income streams Garnod Co's directors must also consider the risks attached to the income streams offered by the two targets.Pilsur Co's revenues have not been growing quickly recently,but the company operates in areas where training will always be needed, either to obtain qualifications or to provide professional updates in areas where regulations change regularly.Marhew Co's revenue stream is smaller,the company is less wellestablished and it operates in areas which could grow significantly but which may be vulnerable if the organisations which Marhew Co serves decide to cut back on their training.These risks, together with Marhew Co being unlisted,may make it less easy to value,although this may mean that it is more likely to be undervalued by its management than if it were listed. Shareholders The shareholder base of the two companies must also be considered.All Garnod Co needs to do to obtain a controlling interest in Marhew Co is to buy the shares of two shareholders,Richard and Katy.As Pilsur Co is listed,it is likely to have a larger number of shareholders.It may be more difficult and time-consuming to convince enough shareholders to sell their shares. Post-acquisition operations If it acquires either company,Garnod Co faces the choice of whether to allow it to operate as a quasi-independent entity or completely absorbing it.There are arguments against the quasi-independent model:Pilsur Co because of its lack of success and resistance to change if its directors'atitudes are shared by its staff;Marhew Co because of the agency issues that its staff may be loyal to Richard and Katy rather than Garnod Co.The disparity in size may make it easier to completely absorb Marhew Co,particularly if Richard and Katy become part of Garnod Co's management team and Marhew Co's staff can be shown the attractive opportunities available in a larger firm. Garnod Co's directors will also need to consider the compatibility of its culture with its two targets.It appears that Marhew Co may be similar to Garnod Co a few years ago,with a few dedicated individuals leading the company forward strongly.However, the culture of Marhew Co is very informal and the company seems to lack the corporate governance mechanisms which Garnod Co has had to develop. (c)Friendly bidder The board of Pilsur Co fears the loss of independence and changes which acquisition by Garnod Co may bring and,in particular,the threat to their own jobs.Under best corporate governance practice,they must act in accordance with the best interests of Pilsur Co's shareholders,rather than their own interests.Attempting to find another buyer to bid may be viewed as legitimate if the bid is higher. However,Pilsur Co operates in a specialist area and is listed on the smaller company investment market rather than the main market,perhaps making it less appealing as an investment.It is quite possible that a friendly bidder may not be found. If Pilsur Co's directors fail to find one,this may persuade its shareholders to accept a lower price from Garnod Co.Also given Pilsur Co's lack of recent growth,the acquirer may feel it has to make significant changes to improve the business's prospects, even if it says when it bids that it does not plan major changes. Counter-bid for Garnod Co If directors are acting in the best interests of Pilsur Co,any bid for Garnod Co should have strategic logic to it.There are strategic reasons in favour of Pilsur Co bidding for Garnod Co:diversification into areas of training which have shown better growth than Pilsur Co's current main areas and also acquiring Garnod Co's e-learning capabilities.The directors of Pilsur Co would remain in charge of the business.However,if Pilsur Co has not been planning an acquisition,it is doubtful whether it has the strategic plans necessary to make the merger work.It may also face resistance from Garnod Co's staff who see themselves as being taken over by a less successful company with a weaker culture. There is also the question of financing the bid.The fact that Pilsur Co's profits have not been growing quickly recently suggests that it may lack the surplus cash required to finance the acquisition.A share-for-share offer may not appear attractive to Garnod Co's shareholders.If the bid is for cash,obtaining equity or debt finance may be difficult f fund providers doubt whether the acquisition will be successful.Pilsur Co also has some debt capital already,so there is also the issue of how feasible it would be to raise further loan capital for any purposes and what the reaction of existing debt providers would be. Q1-A Suggested Solution: (a) Reasons why acquisitions fail Acquisitions may fail due to a variety of reasons,many of which are specific to that particular acquisition.However,there are three broad recurring themes as to why acquisitions have not been successful. First,the strategic reason for the acquisition may be misguided,and thereby could be value destroying.For example,a company may decide to focus on a business area which may face competition in the future,may face economic challenges or be susceptible to technological advances. Second,when assessing the synergy benefit arising from an acquisition,the management may be over-optimistic about the competitive position of the combined company,the economic conditions,the possible cost synergies which could be achieved and/or the sources of other synergy benefits.Also,the costs associated with the acquisition process both before the acquisition,and after the acquisition has taken place,may be underestimated.Costs may also arise due to problems which come to light only after the acquisition has taken place due to poor due diligence processes. Finally,in the excitement to ensure that the acquisition does take place,an excessive price may be paid for the acquisition. Third,and probably the most important reason,is that the management of the integration process after the acquisition has taken place is poor.For example, previously prepared plans may be adhered to too rigidly.Poor communication or over- bearing attitudes from the acquiring company's management could destroy trust and result in key personnel leaving the target company.Additionally,the culture within the target company and its systems may be so different that the acquirer has little option but to operate both companies as separate entities and there would be little opportunity for integration.This in turn may limit any synergy benefits which can be achieved. Steps Kedington Co can take The possible steps Kedington Co could take to ensure that the acquisition is successful include the following. Having a clear rationale for its strategy to expand into the new areas of Boulge and ensuring that the acquisition of Metfield Co is the best way to achieve this.Ensure that the team tasked with the acquisition have appropriate knowledge of the industry and of Metfield Co.A clear and focused investigation of the strategic rationale should be undertaken and the results of this should be discussed thoroughly. A thorough investigation of synergy benefits and all the costs involved should be undertaken,together with detailed due diligence.If the acquisition is not opposed by the target company,its management could be involved in the initial assessment. Sensitivity analysis and/or scenario analysis should be undertaken involving different economic conditions and competitor behaviour to assess best-case and worse-case possibilities.The acquisition team must have a clear maximum bid value and they need to ensure that they do not bid above this. Detailed,but flexible,post-acquisition plans need to be developed and normally the implementation should be quick.This could involve clear communication,a willingness to listen to concerns of the target company's management and involving third parties as mediators.Additionally,it is important to have a post-acquisition audit or review process and procedures should be put into place to respond to any relevant findings. Where corporate cultures are very different between the acquirer and the target,a higher degree of autonomy may be allowed initially,even if this results in lower synergistic benefits initially. (Note:Credit will be given for alternative and relevant points) (b)Report to the board of directors(BoD),Kedington Co This report considers the estimated impact from the proposed acquisition of Metfield Co to both companies'shareholders,based on a cash offer and on a share-for-share offer.It also discusses the possible reaction of each group of shareholders to the two offers. The appendices to the report show the detailed calculations in estimating the equity value of Metfield Co,the expected additional value resulting from acquisition,the number of shares exchanged in the case of the share-for-share offer,and the value to the two companies under each offer method.The funding requirement if the acquisition is to be paid for in cash is also calculated. Summary of results from the appendices Estimate of the current value of a Metfield Co share (appendix one):$1 ·60/share Estimate of the expected total additional value resulting from the acquisition(appendix two):$72m Estimate of the number of Kedington Co shares exchanged for Metfield Co shares in the share-for-share offer(appendix three):two Kedington Co shares for five Metfield Co shares Estimate of the percentage gain in value Kedington Co Metfield Co Cash offer(appendix four) 16.4% 15.0% Share-for-share offer(appendix four) 13.1% 29.5% (iv) Assumptions made The calculations in appendices one and two make a number of assumptions.For example,in order to calculate the estimated share value of Metfield Co,the free cash flow valuation model is used.For this,the growth rate,the cost of capital and the effective time period when the growth will take place (perpetuity in this case)are all estimates.For the acquisition offer,the synergy-related savings and the P/E ratio used in appendix two are both assumptions.The calculations in the appendices do not consider any synergy benefits related to Kedington Co,nor do they consider any impact on its own P/E ratio,as a result of the acquisition.These may increase the value of the acquisition. Reaction of shareholders Notwithstanding the above,it is more likely that Metfield Co's shareholders will opt for the share-for-share offer as the gain in value or the premium is at the higher end of the normal premium offered in similar acquisitions in the retail industry.It is possible that Metfield Co's shareholders will be less attracted to the cash offer as the gain in value or premium is at the lower end of the normal premium offered in similar acquisitions in the retail industry.However,the possibility of a definite cash gain against the uncertain gain in share value resulting from synergy benefits and P/E ratio estimates may be attractive to some shareholders.Metfield Co may want to consider rejecting the cash offer initially and try to negotiate a higher cash offer.However, Kedington Co may not be able to increase the cash offer due to limited availability of funds. Kedington Co's shareholders would gain more if the cash offer was accepted by Metfield Co compared to the share-for-share offer.Additionally,if that offer were accepted,it may increase the reputation of Kedington Co's BoD since the premium offered is below the industry's lower end figure.The share-for-share offer may also be acceptable to them,because even though it gives a lower value (by about 20%),it stops cash outflows from the company,and funds can be made available for alternative investments. It is possible that a lower premium together with a mixed offer of cash and shares may be more acceptable to both sets of shareholders and this possibility should be explored further. (v) Implications of reduction in dividends The funds needed for the cash payment,if that is the preferred option,will in part come from the forecast cash which is expected to be generated from the cash flows from normal business activities.However,this would lead to an 80%reduction in the dividends paid and could have implications to Kedington Co.Modigliani and Miller's theory on dividend policy suggests that corporate value is not affected by the dividend policy of a company because it suggests that shareholders could formulate their own 'home-made'dividend policy by buying and selling shares as needed.Instead,this theory suggests that companies should focus on the investment policies which lead to the creation of corporate value. However,in practice,it is suggested that the amount of dividends paid and the dividend policy adopted by a company does matter because of two main reasons. First,dividends are used by companies as a signalling device to the markets and unexpected changes to a dividend policy are not generally viewed positively by the markets and investors,as they could interpret this as the company failing.Kedington Co will need to be clear in its communication to the markets and the investors that the change in dividends paid is triggered by its investment strategy and not due to any sort of corporate failure. Second,a stable dividend policy attracts certain groups of shareholders,known as the clientele,to the company.This is mainly to do with the personal taxation which the shareholders face.For example,higher rate taxpayers may prefer low dividend payouts and lower rate tax payers may prefer higher dividend payouts.A change in the dividends paid may trigger a change of the clientele of the company,resulting in excessive,and possibly negative,share price volatility. The narrative does not explain why Kedington Co could not raise funds through new equity or debt issuances,nor does it explain why it is necessary to fund part of the acquisition by restricting dividends.A mixed offer may make it unnecessary to obtain funding by restricting dividends.The BoD should consider all these alternatives before making the decision to restrict the amount of dividends paid. Conclusion Metfield Co's shareholders would probably opt for the share-for-share offer as the premium offered here is at the top end of what is offered for acquisitions in the same industry.A lower premium,with a mixed offer of cash and shares,may also be attractive to them.The BoD needs to explore alternative funding possibilities before making the final decision so that dividends are not restricted unnecessarily.However, prior to accepting the offer,Metfield Co's shareholders would need to be assured of the accuracy of the results produced by the computations in the appendices. Q2-A Suggested Solution-Darmsden Co (a) Using futures contracts A dollar payment is being made in five months'time and Darmsden Co needs to hedge against the Euro weakening.It needs to sell Euro futures and use six-month futures contracts. Number of contracts =$7,718,500/1.1229/125,000=54·99 contracts,approximately 55 contracts Basis Assume that basis reduces to zero at the contract maturity in a linear fashion. Basis =Spot price-Futures price =1.1199-1.1229=-0.0030 Unexpired basis =-0.0030×1/6=-0.0005 Lock-in rate =Opening futures price +Unexpired basis Lock-in rate =1.1229-0.0005=1.1224 Expected payment =$7,718,500/1 ·1224=€6,876,782 Alternative method Assume that the spot rate in 1 December is 1.1215. Expected futures price to buy €futures =Expected spot rate-Unexpired basis =1.1215-(-0.0005)=1.1220 Gain on futures market=(1.1229-1.1220)×55× ∈ 125,000=$6,188 Convert $7,718,500 payment on spot market at assumed spot price of 1.1215= $7,718,500/1.1215=$6,882,300 Therefore futures outcome =$6,882,300-$6,188=$6,876,112 Using option contract A$payment is being made in five months'time and Darmsden Co needs to hedge against the Euro weakening.As Darmsden Co is being offered a $option,it should purchase a five-month call option. Exercise price $1.1225/1 Payment =$7,718,500/1.1225 =€6,876,169 Premium =$112,500/1.1199 =€100,455 Total payment =€6,876,169+€100,455=€6,976,624 Exercise price $1.1175/Æ1 Payment =$7,718,500/1.1175 =€6,906,935 Premium =$92,000/1.1199=E82,150 Total payment =€6,906,935+E82,150=€6,989,085 Discussion Using the exchange-traded futures contracts would mean that Darmsden Co would pay less than under either of the option contracts.The issue of futures contract amounts being standardised and not matching Darmsden Co's requirements is not so important here,as the amount hedged is for almost an exact number of contracts. However,transaction costs of the futures contracts have been ignored.The contracts will also require margin payments.There is also basis risk -the risk that the actual basis at the close-out date will differ from the expected basis used in the hedge calculation.This may mean the result of the futures hedge is different to what is expected,although the difference may well be small and still mean that Darmsden Co pays less using futures. Of the two option contracts,the option contract with the exercise price of $1.1225/E1 gives a lower total payment,which Darmsden Co knows is the maximum payment which it will have to make.If Darmsden Co wishes to choose the 1.1225 contract for that reason,it willlet the option lapse if exchange rates move favourably. Darmsden Co might also consider using traded options,to see whether they give a more favourable result than over-the-counter options.Traded options would have the advantage of being traded on a regulated exchange,with greater price transparency and possibly a lower risk of counterparty default,depending on who is offering the over-the-counter options.Darmsden Co could also consider what outcome a forward contract would give,although this would also be subject to counterparty risk. In conclusion,futures should be chosen as Darmsden Co should pay less using futures and they offer the security of being exchange-traded. (b) Initial margin =$1,325×55=$72,875 Day 1 $/Æ Sell-initial transaction 1.1229 Settlement price-Day 1 1.1247 Loss 0.0018 Loss in ticks =0·0018/0·0001=18 Total loss =18 ticks x$12·50×55 contracts =$12,375 Balance on margin account =$72,875-$12,375=$60,500 Darmsden Co would have to deposit an extra $12,375 to increase the balance back to the initial margin. Day 2 $/E Settlement price-Day 1 1.1247 Settlement price-Day 2 1.1236 Gain 0.0011 Gain in ticks =0·0011/0·0001=11 Total profit =11 ticks x$12·50×55 contracts =$7,563 Balance on margin account =$72,875+$7,563=$80,438 Darmsden Co would have to deposit an initial amount of $72,875 for the 55 contracts which it had opened.Its brokers would then calculate the notional profit or loss at each day's settlement price,adjusting the margin account for this and also for any actual transactions. Because Darmsden Co made a notional loss which took it below its initial margin on Day 1,it would have to pay a variation margin to bring its account up to the initial margin.This is not an issue on Day 2. If Darmsden Co did not pay the variation margin for Day 1,its account would only be sufficient to support a hedge of $60,500/$1,325=45 contracts rounding down to the nearest whole contract.Therefore the broker would buy back ten contracts,so that Darmsden Co's hedge would be reduced and the transaction would be underhedged.As the margin account is stated in$,Darmsden Co also needs to consider the impact of foreign exchange risk on payments into the margin account to restore the initial margin. Although Darmsden Co will lose the use of the funds used for the initial margin and may have to make further payments,as on Day 1,the amounts involved are unlikely to be significant if futures prices are quite stable.The requirement to maintain a margin is unlikely to change the decision in (a)about using futures. (b) Comments on measures NPV The only doubts about project 3 relates to the assumptions made and the uncertainties surrounding the cash flow figures,bearing in mind that the value calculated is an expected,not certain,value.The cash flows before tax fall significantly in year 4 and this raises the question of whether they might start falling earlier.The other projects may be able to sustain their cash flows for a longer period, meaning that one of the other projects may have a higher NPV if the time horizon was more than 4 years. The assumptions include using a discount factor related to the business risk characteristics of project 3,by incorporating in the discount factor calculation the asset beta relating to the fruit drinks sector.The sector beta factor may be based on historical data and be of limited relevance for project 3.The sector beta factor may also hide significant differences in the beta factors of different companies within the sector and not be particularly relevant to project 3's circumstances. The uncertainties surrounding the estimates for project 3 are indicated by the value at risk,which is higher than for the other two projects. Duration Duration as a measure of risk may be one way of ranking projects 1 and 2 given that their NPVs are the same.Using duration suggests that project 1 is marginally preferable to project 2.Setting a duration target is subjective,but has some legitimacy if directors are worried about the risks of a project and hence wish to see the investment largely recovered in the short-term.Duration has the theoretical advantages of incorporating all the project cash flows and the time value of money.It can be used to distinguish between projects by showing when a significant percentage of the project's investment will be recovered. VAR The value at risk (VAR)method offers an indication of the dispersion of possible results that the other methods do not offer.However,the VAR target is set subjectively and needs to be consistent with the board's risk appetite.It is dependent assumption that possible results are normally distributed,which may not be true ifon a reliable estimate of the standard deviation.The VAR is also based on the assumption that possible results are normally distributed,which may not be true if the market is volatile.Although here the VAR method appears to indicate that project the market is volatile.Although here the VAR method appears to indicate that project 2 is preferable to project 1 on the grounds that it has a higher probability of avoiding a loss,this takes no account of upside risk-project 1's higher standard deviation suggesting its NPV will be greater if results meet optimistic estimates. Other factors Particularly since the financial indicators are not clear-cut,the board may decide to ignore the fact that projects 1 and 2 do not fulfil the criteria,and choose one or both of them on the grounds that they have positive NPVs.A variety of criteria may be into account include compatibility with longer-term strategic objectives,which of theused to determine the choice between the two.Possible criteria the board willtake into account include compatibility with longer-term strategic objectives,which of the projects it will be operationally easier to undertake alongside project 3,possibility of projects it will be operationally easier to undertake alongside project 3,possibility of cash flows beyond the period of investment appraisal and the existence of real options,particularly follow-on or expansion options. (ii) Reasons why forecast operating synergies may not be realised Firstly,the forecast synergies may not be achievable whatever steps are taken to realise them.Insufficient information available about Tincleton Co may have led to synergies being over-estimated or market conditions may have been viewed over- optimistically. However,there are other possible reasons for not achieving operating synergies which can be addressed.Failure to incorporate Tincleton Co satisfactorily within the strategic framework of Walditch Co willmake it unlikely that long-term synergies can be achieved.Insufficient time and money may be invested to make the acquisition work.Inadequate plans or failure to adapt plans to changing circumstances are likely to cause failure. There may also be conflicts between the cultures or management styles of the two companies.Poor management of the employees will be a major obstacle to achieving synergies,particularly as Tincleton Co appears to have built up a successful and loyal team.This could include showing a lack of respect for Tincleton Co's previous achievements and products.It also includes poor communication of goals and future prospects of employees. Some of these factors,for example strategic planning and communication,may have been problems with the infrastructure support services division.Walditch Co's board should consider whether there are lessons to be learned from how the infrastructure support services division was managed,before planning the integration of Tincleton Co. Steps to realise synergies The first stage is to make further analysis of the sources of synergies and the actions required to realise them.Once the acquisition has been agreed,assistance can be sought from Tincleton Co's management. A clear integration plan needs to be drawn up covering issues such as dealing with staff concerns and resistance,data reporting arrangements and the reporting relationships while the integration takes place.The plan should be sufficiently flexible to be adapted if required during the integration process.The plan should include designation of a senior person to oversee the acquisition,who can command the respect and confidence of both boards. Longer-term decisions will be required about how Tincleton Co willfit into Walditch Co's organisational structure.The degree of autonomy it is allowed may depend on whether Tincleton Co's previous directors will be involved in management in future. Involvement of the previous directors and greater autonomy may make the acquisition more acceptable to Tincleton Co's staff,but it may mean that the changes necessary to achieve synergies are more difficult to impose. Whatever decisions are taken about organisational structure,employees are more likely to be motivated to achieve the synergies if information about what is happening is communicated to them promptly.Staff willbe motivated by communication of benefits to their careers and performance incentives. There also needs to be full integration of Tincleton Co into Walditch Co's strategic planning,particularly the technology capabilities which the acquisition could give to Walditch Co.The technology expertise on Walditch Co's board may need to be enhanced,perhaps by including one of Tincleton Co's directors on the board. (iv) Share exchange The assumption is that shareholders will act rationally in preferring the higher value share exchange offer to the cash offer.A share exchange would allow cash to be retained within Walditch Co for the forecast additional investment required,or for investment in other opportunities. If the share exchange offer is fully taken up it will mean the introduction of a significant bloc of new shareholders.This may have some impact on the balance of shareholder power at Walditch Co,although none of the new shareholders will hold a significant %of shares individually.Walditch Co's shareholders will also be concerned about dilution of earnings per share if the share exchange offer is taken up. Walditch Co's shareholders may also query how the 5 for 6 share exchange offer terms were determined.There is no evidence that it was based on fixed proportions of the added value from the acquisition going to both sets of shareholders.Walditch Co's shareholders may object to the predicted gain to them of 2.4%(($20.89/$20.41) ×100%)being lower than the gain for Tincleton Co's shareholders. Cash However,Tincleton Co's shareholders may choose to take the cash offer.The percentage gain from the cash offer is only marginally lower than the percentage gain from the share exchange and it does offer certainty of return and a convenient exit route if Tincleton Co's shareholders do not want to be shareholders in Walditch Co.A cash offer of $20,760m(1,200 million×$17.30)would use all the funds generated from the sale of the infrastructure support services division and require additional funding,and it is not known what funding would be available to Walditch Co. Walditch Co's shareholders may feel that the cash offer is too high.They may worry about the impact on funds available for other investment opportunities and the implications of having to obtain additional funding if necessary.However,the benefits which Tincleton Co's shareholders gain will be fixed if they take up a cash offer and do not acquire shares in the combined company.If there are better than expected gains after the acquisition,these willallaccrue to Walditch Co's shareholders. Conclusion It is not clear which offer the shareholders of Tincleton Co are likely to prefer and the impact of the choice on Walditch Co's available funds and shareholdings may be significant.For value to be gained by Walditch Co's shareholders,a well-designed plan to achieve synergies will be required. (b) Walditch Co The main ethical issue from Walditch Co's viewpoint is whether the possible offer to Tincleton Co's directors breaches a fundamental principle of takeovers,equivalent treatment of shareholders.Walditch Co's offering the directors jobs would appear to be defensible on business grounds(possibly helping to realise the synergies). However,it appears that the offers may not be genuine,in which case Walditch Co's directors could be accused of bad faith and lack of honesty.The cash bonuses are more questionable,as it seems difficult to justify them for business reasons,so they could be seen as being additional consideration for the directors'shares,meaning that shareholders are not being treated equally. Tincleton Co Tincleton Co's directors face the issue of a possible conflict between their own personal interests and the interests of Tincleton Co's shareholders.Takeover regulations would not seek to prevent them taking jobs with the merged company if there are sound business reasons.However,they should be transparent about what they have been offered,so any offer from Walditch Co should be disclosed by Tincleton Co's directors.They should follow local takeover rules in making these disclosures.The directors who receive the offers should perhaps not be involved in further discussions about the takeover once the offers have been made.
